_meta.description: Consolidated v1.3 bilingual strategies with long definitions. Contains 14 strategies from available chunks.

speaking: Students will orally {function} {content_focus} using {supports} (e.g., {frames}), producing {product} that meets {criteria}.
Students will discuss {content_focus} in pairs, using {frames} to {function} and incorporate {target_vocab}.
listening: Students will comprehend {input_type} on {content_focus} and demonstrate understanding by {response_mode} using {supports}.
Students will follow {procedural_input} and {function} (e.g., sequence, identify) the steps for {content_focus}.
reading: Students will read {text_type} about {content_focus} and {function} (e.g., identify, infer) key ideas using {strategy} and {target_vocab}.
Students will use {strategy} (e.g., cognates, text features) to make meaning from {text_type} about {content_focus}.
writing: Students will write a {genre} to {purpose} about {content_focus}, using {frames}/{target_vocab} and {language_features}.
Students will revise a short paragraph to {function} (e.g., explain, justify) understanding of {content_focus} using {criteria}.

speaking+listening: In structured talk, students will {function} about {content_focus}, using {frames} while peers listen and {response_mode}.
reading+writing: Students will read {text_type} on {content_focus} and write a {genre} that {purpose}, using {target_vocab} and {criteria}.
all: Through {framework}, students will listen to {input_type}, discuss {content_focus}, read {text_type}, and write a {genre} that {purpose}, with {supports}.

strategies.core_principle: Leverage L1–L2 dictionaries to clarify new terms and support independent comprehension.
applicable_contexts: Content lessons with dense academic vocabulary
Writing tasks requiring precise word choice
implementation_steps: Model context-first inference, then confirm with a bilingual dictionary.
Embed routine look-ups in reading/writing; teach efficient dictionary skills.
Verify fit-in-context (e.g., reverse-translation or image check).
primary_benefits: Builds bilingual vocabulary and learner autonomy
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: Purposeful use of L1–L2 dictionaries/glossaries to clarify meaning, verify usage, and build independent vocabulary learning habits.
definitions.long_en: Bilingual Dictionary and Glossary Use is a structured routine where students consult L1–L2 resources to confirm meaning, usage, and collocations of key terms while reading or writing. Instruction emphasizes context-first inference, then verification with a dictionary to avoid overreliance on literal translation. Teachers model efficient lookup behaviors (e.g., scanning multiple senses, checking part of speech, reverse-translation, and confirming with an image or example sentence). Students embed brief lookups during reading, note synonyms and cognates, and test fit by substituting the word back into the sentence. In writing, they use dictionaries to refine precision (e.g., academic versus everyday terms) and check morphology. This strategy increases comprehension of dense texts, develops metalinguistic awareness, and fosters learner autonomy. Use it in content areas with specialized vocabulary or when precision matters; avoid turning it into a stop-every-line routine that disrupts fluency. Set clear time limits, teach when to skip low-importance words, and always confirm that the selected term makes sense in context.
look_fors: Students infer from context, then verify with the dictionary
Teacher models checking part of speech and multiple senses
Reverse-translation or example sentence used to confirm fit
Brief, timed lookups that do not disrupt reading flow
non_examples: Translating every unknown word line-by-line
Choosing the first dictionary entry without checking context
Using dictionaries as a substitute for teaching word learning strategies

strategies.core_principle: Display key terms in English and L1 to make vocabulary constantly visible and meaningful.
implementation_steps: Co-create bilingual word cards with visuals; organize by theme or alphabet.
Routinely reference during instruction and practice via quick games.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: Dual-language word walls and labels that pair key terms with visuals to make academic vocabulary continually visible and usable.
definitions.long_en: Bilingual Word Walls and Labels display high-utility academic and content-specific terms in English alongside students’ L1 equivalents, often with visuals, morphology cues, or example frames. Teachers co-create the wall with students, adding words during unit launches and revisiting it daily in mini-routines (quick reads, choral responses, sort-and-match, or ‘use-it-in-a-sentence’). Cards may be organized by theme, concept map, or part of speech; color-coding highlights cognates or affixes. During lessons, teachers point to the wall to prompt accurate retrieval, help students choose precise terms in speaking and writing, and reinforce spelling and pronunciation. Because the wall lives in the room, it supports independence, inclusion, and repeated exposure—key drivers of vocabulary growth. Use it in vocabulary-heavy units and literacy centers; keep it curated and legible. Avoid cluttered, static displays that are never referenced; the wall must be interactive and aligned to current learning goals to remain effective.
look_fors: Co-created bilingual cards with visuals or morphology cues
Daily reference during instruction and practice
Organized, current terms tied to the unit focus
Students using wall terms in speaking and writing
non_examples: A dense, outdated wall never referenced in lessons
Random long lists with no visuals or organization
Using only English labels in bilingual classrooms

strategies.core_principle: Teach learning strategies (metacognitive, cognitive, social) alongside content.
implementation_steps: Set content + strategy goals; model with think-alouds.
Guide practice (e.g., summarizing, graphic organizers) and reflect on transfer.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: A strategy-based framework that teaches metacognitive, cognitive, and social strategies alongside content to build academic language.
definitions.long_en: CALLA (Cognitive Academic Language Learning Approach) is a structured framework that integrates explicit teaching of learning strategies—metacognitive (plan, monitor, evaluate), cognitive (summarize, infer, organize), and social/affective—into content instruction. Teachers name the strategy purpose, model it with a think‑aloud tied to content objectives, and guide students in applying the strategy with supports (e.g., graphic organizers, sentence frames). Lessons end with reflection on how the strategy helped and when to transfer it to new tasks. Over time, students internalize a repertoire for approaching complex texts and problems while growing academic language functions. CALLA is well suited to upper-elementary and secondary classes, or any lesson demanding comprehension of dense input or multistep reasoning. It is not a grab-bag of tips; effectiveness depends on deliberate cycles of modeling, guided practice, application, and reflection. Avoid overloading students with many strategies at once—teach a small set deeply and spiral their use across units.
look_fors: Explicit naming of the strategy and its purpose
Teacher think‑aloud that models the strategy on content
Guided practice before independent application
Student reflection on when/how to use the strategy
non_examples: Unlabeled use of organizers without strategy language
Introducing several new strategies in a single lesson
Skipping reflection on transfer to future tasks

strategies.core_principle: Plan for simultaneous content objectives and language objectives with scaffolding.
implementation_steps: State content + language objectives; pre-teach key terms.
Use visuals/realia/organizers; build interactive tasks using target language.
primary_benefits: Promotes content mastery and language growth
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: An integrated approach that plans simultaneous content and language objectives with scaffolding in authentic subject lessons.
definitions.long_en: Content and Language Integrated Learning (CLIL) is an instructional approach where subject content (e.g., science, history) is taught through the target language, with explicit planning for language objectives and scaffolds. Teachers identify the disciplinary concepts and the language functions/forms needed to express them (e.g., compare, cause–effect, passive voice), pre-teach key vocabulary, and design tasks that require purposeful language use to solve problems or present findings. Lessons leverage visuals, realia, models, and structured interaction (think‑pair‑share, expert groups) to balance cognitive challenge and language support. Assessment addresses both content understanding and language growth. CLIL is effective in immersion and mainstream settings because it situates language learning in meaningful, knowledge-rich contexts. It should not be reduced to content coverage with incidental language; without planned objectives and scaffolding, learners may grasp ideas but lack the language to express them. Ensure every activity specifies the targeted language function and provides supports for success.
look_fors: Stated content and language objectives
Pre-teaching of key vocabulary and functions
Tasks that require producing target language for content goals
Use of visuals/realia and structured interaction
non_examples: Teaching subject content with no language objectives
Open discussion with high cognitive load but no scaffolds
Assessing only content, ignoring language evidence

strategies.core_principle: Make explicit links between cognates (e.g., informação–information) and teach false cognates.
implementation_steps: Chart L1–L2 cognates and revisit in units.
Design recognition and sorting tasks; teach common false pairs.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: Explicit teaching of L1–L2 cognates and false cognates to accelerate vocabulary growth and metalinguistic insight.
definitions.long_en: Cognate Awareness makes cross-language connections visible by teaching students to recognize, verify, and use cognates (and avoid false cognates). Instruction highlights common roots, affixes, and sound–spelling patterns between L1 and English (e.g., informação–information, energia–energy), and builds routines for checking meaning in context. Students sort and chart cognate families, annotate texts to mark likely cognates, and practice using them in speaking and writing with attention to part of speech and morphology. Teachers also surface frequent false pairs (e.g., “pasta” vs. “folder”) to reduce misconceptions. This accelerates vocabulary acquisition in content reading, strengthens decoding of academic terms with Greco‑Latin roots, and deepens metalinguistic awareness. Use it to launch new units or when texts contain many technical terms; avoid assuming that all look‑alike words are cognates or focusing only on translation without contextual verification. Always pair cognate work with meaning-making activities so words are learned as concepts, not isolated items.
look_fors: Charts or organizers grouping cognate families
Students marking candidate cognates while reading
Attention to false cognates and context verification
Use of cognates in speaking/writing with correct form
non_examples: Treating all look‑alike words as true cognates
Isolated lists without application in reading/writing
Skipping instruction on false cognates

strategies.core_principle: Compare English and L1 structures to address predictable errors and build awareness.
implementation_steps: Present side-by-side examples; state rules in both languages.
Practice with translation/correction tasks; reference during feedback.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: Teaching students to compare L1 and English structures to anticipate challenges and highlight transferable knowledge.
definitions.long_en: Contrastive Analysis is the systematic comparison of linguistic structures in students’ L1 and in English to highlight similarities and differences. Teachers guide students to notice phonological, morphological, syntactic, or pragmatic features that transfer positively (e.g., cognates, similar word order) and those that may cause errors (e.g., false cognates, article use). In practice, teachers present side‑by‑side examples, chart similarities, and explicitly point out potential pitfalls. Students practice transforming sentences across languages and reflect on how knowledge of L1 helps anticipate English patterns. This fosters metalinguistic awareness and prevents fossilization of common errors. Contrastive analysis is especially effective when introducing complex grammar, writing conventions, or academic registers. It should not devolve into prescriptive ‘avoid mistakes’ lists; instead, it should celebrate cross‑linguistic resources while clarifying non‑equivalences.
look_fors: Side‑by‑side L1–English examples on board or chart
Discussion of both transferable features and pitfalls
Students transforming or translating short sentences
Reflection on how L1 knowledge informs English learning
non_examples: Only error‑correction without positive transfer
Focusing exclusively on L1 interference as deficit
Teaching rules in isolation without contrast

strategies.core_principle: Use structured peer interaction, allowing L1 discussion toward English output.
implementation_steps: Group same-L1 peers for comprehension in L1; require English output products.
Provide protocols and sentence frames; monitor and debrief.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: Structured peer interaction that promotes negotiation of meaning, language practice, and co-construction of knowledge.
definitions.long_en: Collaborative Learning involves structured peer activities in which students jointly engage in tasks that require both academic thinking and language use. Typical formats include think‑pair‑share, jigsaw reading, expert groups, and project-based group work. Teachers set clear roles, provide sentence frames, and monitor to ensure equitable participation. Collaboration prompts students to negotiate meaning, clarify concepts, and use academic language functions such as justifying, comparing, or predicting. It supports both comprehension (listening to peers) and production (speaking/writing to contribute). Because peers often share linguistic resources, bilingual explanations can surface naturally and can be bridged to English output. Collaborative learning is powerful for engagement and oral language development but requires explicit norms; without structure, some students may dominate while others remain silent. Teachers must circulate, scaffold language, and hold groups accountable for both process and product.
look_fors: Students working in pairs or groups with clear roles
Use of sentence frames and word banks during discussion
Peer explanations and negotiation of meaning
Teacher monitoring and scaffolding group talk
non_examples: Unstructured group work with unclear goals
One student completing the task for the group
Silent groups working individually without interaction

strategies.core_principle: Use visuals, interactive charts, chants, and collaborative structures to teach content + language.
implementation_steps: Plan pictorial input/observation charts; co-construct sentence patterning charts.
Leverage chants and expert groups; revisit charts across the unit.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: Guided Language Acquisition Design integrates thematic units, visuals, and cooperative structures to build language through content.
definitions.long_en: GLAD (Guided Language Acquisition Design) is a professional development model and framework that equips teachers with strategies to deliver content and language instruction simultaneously. GLAD emphasizes thematic unit design, high student engagement, and multimodal input. Signature practices include pictorial input charts, process grids, chants, and cooperative group structures that embed vocabulary and syntax in meaningful contexts. Students interact with visual, oral, and kinesthetic inputs, then produce language through structured output activities. GLAD is often implemented school-wide and pairs well with bilingual programs because it leverages visuals and cooperative learning to scaffold rigorous content. It should not be reduced to isolated tricks; fidelity involves unit-level planning and consistent use of the repertoire. When implemented fully, GLAD sustains both content mastery and robust language development.
look_fors: Use of pictorial input charts and process grids
Structured chants and cooperative activities
Integration of visuals, oral input, and student output
Unit-level coherence of strategies
non_examples: Occasional isolated chart without follow-up
Overuse of chants without content tie-in
Using GLAD elements without unit planning

strategies.core_principle: Brief L1 preview and post-lesson review while main instruction occurs in English.
implementation_steps: Preview key ideas/vocabulary in L1; teach in English; review/clarify in L1.
Invite L1 explanations to check comprehension; recycle target English terms.
primary_benefits: Maintains lesson flow without constant translation
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: Brief L1 preview of key ideas before an English lesson and short L1 review after, reinforcing comprehension.
definitions.long_en: Preview–Review is a bilingual scaffolding strategy where the teacher introduces two or three key concepts and essential vocabulary in students' L1 before delivering the main lesson in English. The preview is short, focused, and supported by visuals or demonstrations. The main instruction proceeds in English, with scaffolds such as sentence frames or manipulatives. At the end, the teacher briefly reviews the concepts in L1, checks understanding, and immediately cycles back to the English terms to strengthen transfer. This approach lowers cognitive load, ensures access to critical ideas, and maintains English as the primary medium. It is especially useful when teaching abstract concepts, launching new units, or clarifying safety directions. The strategy should remain time-bounded and purposeful; if it expands into re-teaching the entire lesson in L1 or continuous translation, its effectiveness is lost.
look_fors: Concise L1 preview with visuals before lesson
English lesson delivered without ongoing translation
Brief L1 review and comprehension check after
English vocabulary recycled immediately after review
non_examples: Full re-teaching of lesson in L1
Continuous sentence-by-sentence translation
Extended L1 discussions unrelated to English output

strategies.core_principle: Framework of best practices to make grade-level content comprehensible while developing academic English.
implementation_steps: State content + language objectives; build background and vocabulary.
Use visuals/interactions; practice & application; review/assess for both domains.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: A comprehensive framework integrating content and language objectives with strategies for making input comprehensible.
definitions.long_en: The Sheltered Instruction Observation Protocol (SIOP) is a validated framework that guides teachers in planning and delivering lessons integrating content and language objectives. It includes eight components: lesson preparation, building background, comprehensible input, strategies, interaction, practice/application, lesson delivery, and review/assessment. Teachers explicitly state and review objectives, teach key vocabulary, and use techniques like visuals, modeling, and cooperative learning to make grade-level content accessible. Students engage in structured opportunities to practice new language orally and in writing. SIOP lessons balance content rigor with language scaffolds, ensuring equity for bilingual learners in mainstream classrooms. The model is widely researched and effective across grade levels. It should not be reduced to a checklist of strategies; fidelity requires coherent planning, reflection, and consistency. When fully implemented, SIOP helps close achievement gaps while accelerating academic English development.
look_fors: Content and language objectives posted and reviewed
Explicit vocabulary instruction with supports
Comprehensible input with visuals and modeling
Multiple opportunities for interaction and practice
non_examples: Teaching content without language objectives
Checklists without coherent planning
Relying on worksheets without interaction

strategies.core_principle: Provide temporary supports (models, visuals, frames) and fade as independence grows.
implementation_steps: Model (I do–We do–You do); use organizers and manipulatives.
Provide sentence frames/word banks; gradually remove supports.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: Temporary supports (visuals, modeling, sentence frames) that enable learners to perform tasks just beyond independent ability.
definitions.long_en: Scaffolding refers to the instructional supports teachers provide to help students accomplish tasks they could not complete independently. Examples include modeling a process, using visuals, providing sentence starters, chunking texts, or guiding practice in small steps. Scaffolds are gradually withdrawn as students gain proficiency, fostering independence. For bilingual learners, scaffolds may include bilingual glossaries, L1 peer support, or translation of key terms, always with the goal of bridging to English academic language. Effective scaffolding requires diagnosing students’ current level, anticipating challenges, and matching supports to objectives. It is a dynamic process: supports must be adjusted based on observation. Scaffolding should not become permanent crutches; overuse can limit independence. Instead, supports fade strategically as students internalize skills and language.
look_fors: Teacher modeling before student practice
Use of visuals, sentence frames, or chunked texts
Gradual release of responsibility
Supports matched to language and content goals
non_examples: Assigning complex tasks with no supports
Providing permanent sentence frames without fading
One-size-fits-all scaffolds unrelated to objectives

strategies.core_principle: Brief, purposeful switches to L1 to unlock comprehension, then immediate return to English.
implementation_steps: Clarify in L1 in a sentence or two; immediately restate in English.
Monitor frequency and reduce as proficiency grows.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: Planned, purposeful shifts between L1 and English to clarify meaning, build connections, and maintain flow of instruction.
definitions.long_en: Strategic Code-Switching is the intentional use of students’ first language alongside English during instruction to enhance comprehension and learning. Teachers may briefly switch to L1 to clarify a complex idea, emphasize a key point, or check understanding, then immediately return to English. This differs from unplanned translation: it is purposeful, targeted, and time-limited. Strategic code-switching validates students’ linguistic resources while keeping English as the main medium of instruction. For example, a teacher may explain a metaphor in Portuguese before guiding students to re-express it in English. When used judiciously, it lowers anxiety, accelerates transfer, and deepens conceptual understanding. Overuse, however, risks reducing exposure to English and encouraging dependence. The goal is to leverage L1 as a scaffold that bridges to independent English use, not as a replacement for English instruction.
look_fors: Brief, purposeful switches to L1 with clear return to English
Clarification of complex ideas or cultural references in L1
Students re-expressing ideas in English after L1 support
Teacher signaling intentionality of the switch
non_examples: Constant sentence-by-sentence translation
Unplanned shifts to L1 whenever students struggle
Overreliance on L1 with little English practice

strategies.core_principle: Explicitly map learned content/vocabulary from L1 to English (and vice versa) to ensure transfer.
implementation_steps: List key L1 concepts; co-create English equivalents in two-column charts.
Compare cognates/false cognates; apply via English summaries/presentations.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: A structured lesson phase where teachers explicitly connect learning in L1 to English, reinforcing transfer of concepts and language.
definitions.long_en: The Bridge is a component of biliteracy instruction where the teacher deliberately connects concepts learned in students’ L1 to English, highlighting similarities and differences in vocabulary, grammar, and discourse. Typically taught after a unit in L1, the Bridge involves side-by-side comparisons, co-created charts, and discussion of cognates, morphology, and syntax. Students explore how knowledge transfers across languages and practice applying it in English tasks. The Bridge validates L1 learning, strengthens cross-linguistic awareness, and makes transfer visible. It is most powerful when embedded in a systematic biliteracy framework, ensuring that both languages are valued. The Bridge is not a quick translation exercise; it is a reflective process that empowers students to see themselves as biliterate and to apply skills flexibly. Avoid treating it as an afterthought; intentional planning is essential for its impact.
look_fors: Side-by-side charts of L1 and English terms
Discussion of cognates, morphology, and syntax
Students applying knowledge in English after L1 learning
Explicit teacher facilitation of transfer
non_examples: Literal translation of texts without reflection
Skipping the Bridge phase entirely
Focusing only on English without validating L1 learning

strategies.core_principle: Invite students’ full linguistic repertoire for meaning-making and expression.
applicable_contexts: Brainstorming/drafting in L1 before English output
implementation_steps: Offer multilingual resources; allow L1 brainstorming/notes; peer translation tasks.
Bridge to English products with scaffolds and modeling.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: Pedagogy that allows learners to flexibly use their full linguistic repertoires (L1 and English) to learn and express meaning.
definitions.long_en: Translanguaging is a teaching approach that legitimizes and strategically incorporates students’ full linguistic repertoires into classroom learning. It allows learners to use both their L1 and English fluidly for meaning-making, collaboration, and identity expression. In practice, teachers may encourage brainstorming or drafting in L1, peer discussions in mixed languages, or bilingual word walls and resources. Students then bridge back to English for formal outputs, such as essays or presentations. This approach deepens comprehension, affirms cultural identity, and develops metalinguistic awareness. Translanguaging is not the same as random code-switching; it is purposeful, designed with clear objectives, and linked to outcomes in English. Overuse without bridging risks limiting English exposure; the key is balance and intentionality. When implemented thoughtfully, translanguaging transforms bilingualism from a challenge into a resource for learning.
look_fors: Students using L1 resources to support English tasks
Bilingual displays and word walls in classroom
Teacher prompts bridging L1 work to English output
Peer discussions leveraging both languages
non_examples: Teacher translating everything directly into L1
Students using only L1 without bridging to English
No clear objective guiding multilingual use
